content.text: Service Catalog Managed Applications

A Service Catalog managed application is an Azure offering that lets a central platform team (the publisher) package an ARM template into a self-service deployment unit that other teams (consumers) can deploy through the Azure Portal, CLI, or API — without direct access to the underlying template or the resources it creates.

The publisher retains management access to the resources deployed by the managed application, enforcing compliance and preventing drift. This is the key distinction from an ordinary template deployment: in a managed application, the consumer gets a working solution but cannot modify its internals directly.

attrs.alt: Service Catalog managed application publisher vs consumer flow

content.text: Core Concepts
Concept
Description
Application definition
The Microsoft.Solutions/applicationDefinitions resource that stores the package location, allowed authorizations, and lock level
Managed resource group
A separate resource group ARM creates for the deployed resources; the publisher has Owner access, the consumer has read-only or no direct access
Application resource
The Microsoft.Solutions/applications resource created in the consumer's resource group; it links to the managed resource group
createUiDefinition.json
A JSON schema that drives the portal wizard UI for consumer input; controls which parameters appear and how they are validated
mainTemplate.json
The ARM template deployed into the managed resource group; receives values from createUiDefinition outputs
Authorization
A list of AAD principals (users, groups, service principals) with roles on the managed resource group
Package Structure
A managed application package is a .zip file containing exactly two files at the root level. The names are fixed — ARM rejects packages with different filenames or nested directories.

managed-app/
  mainTemplate.json      # ARM template deployed into the managed resource group
  createUiDefinition.json # Portal wizard definition

# Build the package
zip -j managed-app.zip mainTemplate.json createUiDefinition.json

# Upload to blob storage
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name mytemplatesstorage \
  --container-name packages \
  --name managed-app.zip \
  --file managed-app.zip

# Generate SAS URI valid for 1 year (use longer for production)
PACKAGE_URI=$(az storage blob generate-sas \
  --account-name mytemplatesstorage \
  --container-name packages \
  --name managed-app.zip \
  --permissions r \
  --expiry 2026-12-31 \
  --full-uri -o tsv)

Store the package in a storage account with versioned blob storage enabled. When you publish a new version of the managed application, upload a new .zip with a different blob name so consumers can compare versions before upgrading.
Creating the Application Definition

The application definition is created with az managedapp definition create. It references the package URI, specifies the lock level on the managed resource group, and lists which AAD principals get Owner access to the managed resources.

# Get the Object ID of the AAD group that will manage deployed resources
OWNER_GROUP_ID=$(az ad group show \
  --group 'PlatformTeam' \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Built-in Owner role definition ID
OWNER_ROLE_ID='8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635'

# Create the Service Catalog definition
az managedapp definition create \
  --name 'StandardWebApp' \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --lock-level ReadOnly \
  --display-name 'Standard Web Application' \
  --description 'App Service + Key Vault + Storage with org standards baked in' \
  --authorizations "${OWNER_GROUP_ID}:${OWNER_ROLE_ID}" \
  --package-file-uri "$PACKAGE_URI"

The --lock-level flag sets the default lock on the managed resource group. ReadOnly prevents consumers from making any changes; CanNotDelete allows reads and modifications but prevents deletion. Setting it to None is also valid but removes the enforcement guarantee.
Deploying a Managed Application (Consumer)
Via Azure CLI

# Get the definition resource ID
DEF_ID=$(az managedapp definition show \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Deploy the managed application into the consumer's resource group
az managedapp create \
  --name mywebapp \
  --resource-group consumer-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --kind ServiceCatalog \
  --managed-rg-id "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/mywebapp-managed-rg" \
  --managedapp-definition-id "$DEF_ID" \
  --parameters '{"appName": {"value": "myapp"}, "environment": {"value": "prod"}}'
Via ARM Template (Consumer-Side)
createUiDefinition.json

The createUiDefinition.json file drives the portal wizard UI that consumers see when deploying the managed application. It defines which input controls appear, how values are validated, and how inputs map to template parameters.
Listing and Managing Definitions

# List all definitions in a resource group
az managedapp definition list \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --query '[].{name:name,version:tags.version,location:location}' \
  -o table

# Show definition details including package URI
az managedapp definition show \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-rg

# List deployed managed application instances (consumer side)
az managedapp list \
  --resource-group consumer-rg \
  --query '[].{name:name,state:properties.provisioningState,managedRG:properties.managedResourceGroupId}' \
  -o table

# Delete a managed application (also deletes the managed resource group)
az managedapp delete \
  --name mywebapp \
  --resource-group consumer-rg
Updating a Managed Application

Consumers can trigger an update to a managed application by re-running az managedapp create with updated parameters. Publishers update the underlying resources by re-deploying the mainTemplate directly into the managed resource group, which they have Owner access to.

# Publisher: deploy an updated template into the managed resource group
# (Consumer does not need to do anything; changes appear automatically)
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group mywebapp-managed-rg \
  --template-file updated-main.bicep \
  --parameters appName=myapp environment=prod

# Consumer: update parameters on the managed application itself
az managedapp create \
  --name mywebapp \
  --resource-group consumer-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --kind ServiceCatalog \
  --managed-rg-id '/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/mywebapp-managed-rg' \
  --managedapp-definition-id "$DEF_ID" \
  --parameters '{"appName": {"value": "myapp"}, "appServiceSku": {"value": "P1v3"}}'
Bicep-Based Package Authoring
Bicep can be used to author the mainTemplate.json inside the package. Compile the .bicep file first, then bundle the output with createUiDefinition.json.

# Compile Bicep to ARM JSON
az bicep build --file mainTemplate.bicep --outfile mainTemplate.json

# Bundle package
zip -j managed-app-v2.zip mainTemplate.json createUiDefinition.json

# Upload new version
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name mytemplatesstorage \
  --container-name packages \
  --name managed-app-v2.zip \
  --file managed-app-v2.zip \
  --overwrite

# Update the definition to point to the new package
NEW_URI=$(az storage blob generate-sas \
  --account-name mytemplatesstorage \
  --container-name packages \
  --name managed-app-v2.zip \
  --permissions r --expiry 2026-12-31 --full-uri -o tsv)

az managedapp definition update \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --package-file-uri "$NEW_URI"
Access Control: Authorizations and Lock Levels

The authorizations list in the application definition grants specific AAD principals a role on the managed resource group. Multiple authorizations can be specified — for example, granting Contributor to an operations group and Reader to a monitoring group.

# Retrieve multiple group object IDs
OPS_GROUP=$(az ad group show --group 'OpsTeam' --query id -o tsv)
MON_GROUP=$(az ad group show --group 'MonitoringTeam' --query id -o tsv)

# Built-in role IDs
CONTRIBUTOR='b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'
READER='acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7'

az managedapp definition create \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --lock-level ReadOnly \
  --display-name 'Standard Web App v2' \
  --authorizations "${OPS_GROUP}:${CONTRIBUTOR}" "${MON_GROUP}:${READER}" \
  --package-file-uri "$NEW_URI"
Lock Level
Consumer Can Read
Consumer Can Modify
Consumer Can Delete
Use Case
None
Yes
Yes
Yes
Soft enforcement only; consumer has full control
CanNotDelete
Yes
Yes
No
Allow operational changes but prevent accidental deletion
ReadOnly
Yes
No
No
Strict compliance; publisher controls all changes

ReadOnly is the recommended lock level for platform-managed shared services. CanNotDelete works well for managed databases where the consumer may need to tune connection pool settings or configure backups.
Python SDK — Creating Definitions Programmatically
The Python SDK provides the ApplicationDefinitionsOperations client for managing application definitions in CI/CD pipelines.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource.managedapplications import ApplicationClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.managedapplications.models import (
    ApplicationDefinition, ApplicationLockLevel,
    ApplicationProviderAuthorization
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ApplicationClient(credential, subscription_id)

definition = client.application_definitions.begin_create_or_update(
    resource_group_name='platform-rg',
    application_definition_name='StandardWebApp',
    parameters=ApplicationDefinition(
        location='eastus',
        lock_level=ApplicationLockLevel.READ_ONLY,
        display_name='Standard Web Application',
        description='App Service + Key Vault with org defaults',
        package_file_uri=package_sas_uri,
        authorizations=[
            ApplicationProviderAuthorization(
                principal_id=ops_group_object_id,
                role_definition_id='b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c',  # Contributor
            )
        ],
    ),
).result()

print(f'Definition ID: {definition.id}')
Common Pitfalls
Package file names must be exactly mainTemplate.json and createUiDefinition.json at the zip root. Nested directories or different names cause deployment failures with an unhelpful error message.

SAS tokens on the package URI expire. If the SAS URI expires, new deployments will fail with 403. Use long-lived SAS tokens or switch to using a storage account with public access and Azure Policy to restrict who can deploy.

The managed resource group name must be unique within the subscription. If a consumer reuses a name that already exists (e.g. from a previous deletion), the deployment will fail unless the old group was fully deleted.

Outputs from mainTemplate.json are visible in the managed application resource properties. Do not place secrets (connection strings, passwords) in template outputs — use Key Vault references and have the application fetch secrets at runtime instead.
After a consumer deletes a managed application, the managed resource group is also deleted. Ensure any stateful resources (databases, storage) have their own backup strategy before deletion.
Monitoring and Querying Deployed Instances

After consumers deploy managed applications, the platform team can query all instances across subscriptions using Azure Resource Graph to check compliance, track versions, and audit provisioning state.

# List all deployed managed application instances across the tenant
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.solutions/applications'
  | where kind == 'servicecatalog'
  | project name, resourceGroup, location,
      state = properties.provisioningState,
      defId = properties.applicationDefinitionId,
      managedRG = properties.managedResourceGroupId
  | order by resourceGroup asc
" -o table

# Filter to a specific definition
DEF_NAME='StandardWebApp'
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.solutions/applications'
  | where properties.applicationDefinitionId contains '${DEF_NAME}'
  | project name, resourceGroup, state = properties.provisioningState
" -o table
Checking App Service Health in Managed Resource Groups

# For each managed application, check the App Service plan SKU
az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.web/serverfarms'
  | where resourceGroup contains '-managed-rg'
  | project name, resourceGroup,
      sku = sku.name,
      location
" -o table

# Alert if any managed app has fallen into a Failed provisioning state
FAILED=$(az graph query -q "
  Resources
  | where type == 'microsoft.solutions/applications'
  | where properties.provisioningState == 'Failed'
  | project name, resourceGroup
" --query 'data' -o json)

if [ "$FAILED" != '[]' ]; then
  echo 'WARNING: Failed managed application instances:'
  echo "$FAILED" | python3 -m json.tool
fi
Publisher Deployment Script: Updating All Instances

# Publisher utility: redeploy updated template into every managed resource group
import subprocess, json

# Query all instances of the managed application
result = subprocess.run(
    ['az', 'graph', 'query', '-q',
     "Resources | where type == 'microsoft.solutions/applications' "
     "| where properties.applicationDefinitionId contains 'StandardWebApp' "
     "| project managedRG = properties.managedResourceGroupId",
     '-o', 'json'],
    capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
)
instances = json.loads(result.stdout)['data']
print(f'Found {len(instances)} instances to update')

for inst in instances:
    rg = inst['managedRG'].split('/')[-1]  # extract RG name from resource ID
    print(f'  Updating {rg}...')
    subprocess.run(
        ['az', 'deployment', 'group', 'create',
         '--resource-group', rg,
         '--template-file', 'mainTemplate.json',
         '--parameters', 'appServiceSku=P1v3'],
        check=True
    )
    print(f'  Done: {rg}')
Summary

Service Catalog managed applications provide a self-service deployment model where a platform team packages templates into versioned offerings with enforced locks on the managed resource group. Consumers deploy through a portal wizard or CLI without accessing the template directly. The publisher retains Owner-level management access for updates and compliance enforcement. The package contains two fixed files: mainTemplate.json and createUiDefinition.json. Definitions live in a publisher resource group; deployed instances live in the consumer's resource group linked to a separate managed resource group that the consumer cannot modify directly.
